content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN strings consisting of lowercase English letters.

Two strings are called anagrams if they contain the same characters
with the same frequency, possibly in a different order.

Your task is to group all anagrams together.


Input Format

The input consists of multiple lines.

The first line contains a single integer NNN , the number of strings.
The next NNN lines each contain one string consisting of lowercase English letters.




Output Format

Print the grouped anagrams.

Print each group on a new line in order of first occurrence.
Print words of a group in their original order, separated by a single space.
constraints: All strings contain only lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of strings. The next N lines each contain one string consisting only of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the groups of anagrams. Each group is printed on its own line. Words inside a group appear in the same order as in the input, separated by a single space. Groups are ordered by the first occurrence of any of their members in the input. Separate consecutive test cases by a blank line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The strings "eat", "tea" and "ate" share the sorted key "aet" and form the first group. "tan" and "nat" share key "ant" forming the second group. "bat" has a unique key, so it appears alone.
No two strings are anagrams, so each appears in its own group preserving the input order.

solution.approach: For each string compute a canonical key by sorting its characters. Strings with the same key are anagrams. Use a hash map (or unordered_map) that maps the key to a vector storing the strings in their original order. To preserve the order of groups, also keep a list (or vector) of keys in the order they first appear. After reading all strings of a test case, iterate over the stored keys and output the corresponding vectors. The algorithm runs in O(N * L log L) time where L is the maximum string length (sorting each string) and uses O(N * L) memory for storing the strings and the map.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * L log L) per test case, where L ≤ 100.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * L) to store all strings and the grouping map.
